rows: Identify decision type
Need to determine whether a decision is programmed (rule-amenable, fast) or non-programmed (novel, ambiguous, six-step process).
Detect reactive-system processing
A decision is being made under strong emotion or time pressure; reactive system may be short-circuiting reflection.
Surface ethical dimension
A decision affects stakeholders without voice; Rest's four-component model needs to be applied.
Detect escalation of commitment
A prior decision is being defended after evidence has shifted against it.
Apply SWOT / PESTEL / Porter's Five Forces framing
A strategic choice is in front of you; external forces (PESTEL) and competitive structure (Porter) need naming before the internal capability audit (VRIO).
Apply ethical lens to a management decision

A decision involves conduct that may harm stakeholders; one of the seven ethical principles (utilitarian, universalism, rights, justice, virtue ethics, common good, relativism) should be explicitly selected.
Identify whether GAAP permits multiple treatments
A method choice is in front of you; "what we did last year" is being treated as the default rather than as a decision.
Surface the ethical dimension of an accounting choice
Estimate revisions or method changes accumulate in the same direction; the front-page test is needed.
Identify the affected stakeholders for an accounting decision
A judgement call materially affects reported income, balance sheet, ratios, or covenants.
Identify whether a cost is product or period, variable or fixed, relevant or irrelevant
A cost-basis discussion is collapsing multiple cost-behaviour categories; the decision requires separating them.
Surface the full opportunity cost of a proposed action
The decision considers only out-of-pocket costs; contribution margin on displaced alternatives is not on the table.
Surface the opportunity cost
Discussion is anchored on cash outlay; the value of the next-best foregone alternative has not been named.
Detect sunk-cost framing
A choice is being justified by what has already been spent rather than by marginal forward value.
Convert binary framing to marginal
A decision is being treated as all-or-nothing when a smaller pilot, partial commitment, or incremental adjustment would generate more decision-useful information.
Clarify time horizon
Short-run and long-run decision rules differ; the same factors that are fixed in the short run can adjust in the long run.
Apply normative ethical theory to the decision
A decision has an ethical dimension; the correct framework (utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, contractarian) needs to be selected explicitly before evaluating options.
Surface the ethical question in a business decision
A decision is being treated as purely financial or operational; stakeholder harm or benefit has not been named.
Apply the single ethical standard check
Multiple ethical theories are being applied inconsistently; a single-standard audit is needed before committing.
Identify whether a decision is programmed or non-programmed
A manager is applying a routine process to a novel problem; the five-step model (define, identify, select, implement, follow-up) should be invoked.
Identify the opportunity: problem vs opportunity type
A decision is nominally about solutions when the underlying opportunity itself has not been validated.
Surface the customer problem before the product decision
The team is debating product features without grounding in a validated unmet need.
Frame the market segmentation question before the marketing decision
A marketing decision is being made without naming the segment (demographic, psychographic, behavioural, geographic, benefit) or the position relative to competitors.
Surface the environmental forces shaping the decision
A marketing decision is being made without scanning the macroenvironment; PESTLE and microenvironment (5M, suppliers, intermediaries, competitors, publics) need naming.
Define the financing question: time-value frame
A capital or investment decision is being framed without discounting; future cash flows need to be brought to present value.
Surface the risk-return dimension
A financial decision is being evaluated on expected return alone without naming systematic risk, beta, or the discount-rate implication.
Identify the legal dimension of the decision
A business decision involves contract formation, agency, IP, employment, or regulatory compliance; the legal exposure needs to be named before the business choice.
Apply the ADR question early
A dispute is live or anticipated; the four-tier ADR escalation (negotiation, mediation, arbitration, litigation) question should be asked before filing or threatening.
Apply perception-and-attention limits diagnostic
A decision involves reading a complex situation; selective attention, bottom-up vs top-down processing, or perceptual set may be distorting the frame.
Detect attribution error in the decision framing
The team is attributing outcomes to person rather than situation (fundamental attribution error); dispositional framing may be foreclosing situational options.
Recognise folklore-driven decision pattern

The decision rests on a productivity-difference claim (28:1), a complexity metric (Halstead/McCabe), a maintenance-vs-development ratio (5:1+), or a fault-cost growth claim (1:10:100) — check whether the supporting evidence still holds when survival is factored in.
Detect anchoring on customer's number
A bid or estimate has been generated after the customer offered their own figure; the estimate is likely shifted by the anchor. Re-anchor independently.
Name the risk explicitly
A software-project decision is being made without naming the estimated probability of loss; risk should be stated (high/medium/low or numerically) before bounding the decision.
Position the decision in the triple constraint
A software-project decision involves trade-offs among time, cost, and scope; the project priority matrix (Constrain / Enhance / Accept) should be drawn first.
Test urgency vs importance
A task list is being prioritised under "everything is urgent" pressure; the Eisenhower matrix (Do / Decide / Delegate / Delete) reframes the decision.
Decide whether to spike before estimating
The team lacks the information to estimate, prioritise, or design; a Spike (focused investigation) should precede commitment.
Test for "complex problem" framing
Is this a complex problem (high uncertainty, what-will-happen is unknown) — and therefore Scrum's empirical approach fits — or a known/predictable one better served by plan-and-execute?
Identify the appropriate Scrum decision moment
A decision is being made informally when it belongs in Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, or Sprint Retrospective. Route the decision to its event.
Surface decision rights between Product Owner / Developers / Scrum Master

Decision ownership is unclear; PO owns Product Backlog ordering and Sprint cancellation, Developers own sizing and how-to-build, Scrum Master is accountable for the team's decision-making ability but does not own product decisions.
Detect single-owner-of-Product-Backlog violation
The Product Backlog is being shaped by a committee when "the Product Owner is one person, not a committee."
Decide whether the just-culture guide applies
A patient-safety incident has surfaced a concern about an individual action; the manager-to-manager conversation needs the structured anchor before formal action is contemplated.
Make default-to-system explicit before evaluation
The conversation is drifting toward individual blame as the default; the source's prior — "action singling out an individual is rarely appropriate" — needs to be on the table.
Decide which learning tool fits the unintended outcome

An accident, near-miss, or surprising non-accident has occurred; choose between AAR (closed/local), RLS (shareable), FLA (delegated, no recommendations), and Learning Review (focus groups + Safety Action Plan). Scale by learning opportunity, not severity of outcome.
Frame the analysis by conditions, not causes
The discussion is anchoring on a root cause; reframe as *conditions that influenced the outcome* (networked causality). "FLAs must avoid causal statements."
Detect fundamental attribution error
Blame is being directed at the individual at the sharp end; reframe as the blunt-end conditions that shaped the work.
Detect the "we just need more training" reflex
Training is being prescribed as the default response; sometimes that's the answer, often the issue is work-system design making the work impossible to do safely while following procedure.
Re-frame post-event review from grading to discovery
A review is being convened that will inform a next decision; surface the AAR-vs-critique choice before designing the agenda — grading produces self-defence, discovery produces candour.
Identify our equivalent of fratricide
Identify in advance the events whose severity overrides normal time-budget discipline (near-misses, safety-of-life events, ethics breaches); name them before they happen.
Identify the right Scrum event for this decision

A decision is being made informally when it belongs in a specific Scrum event (Sprint Planning for commitment, Daily Scrum for today's plan, Sprint Review for what-next with stakeholders, Sprint Retrospective for process change, Backlog Refinement for what's Ready/prioritised). Route the decision to the right event before deciding.
Detect status-meeting drift in the Daily Scrum or Sprint Review
The Daily Scrum is being treated as a report to PO/SM/stakeholders, or the Sprint Review is being treated as a presentation rather than working session. Both corrupt decision quality.
Distinguish outcome decisions from implementation decisions

Backlog Items are mixing *what* (PO/stakeholder territory, outcome from customer's perspective) with *how* (Development Team's territory, implementation). The coffee-temperature example as the template.
Check the Retrospective Prime Directive posture

If this is a retrospective decision, does the team's posture honour the Prime Directive ("everyone did the best job they could given what they knew")? Without that frame, the analysis tilts toward blame.
Recognise the chain-of-fit decision rule for an org-design question
An org-change decision is being framed without naming the business strategy it must deliver. The chain-of-fit rule (business strategy → org goal → topology → frameworks) is the framing test.
Detect framework-first reasoning in an org-change proposal
The proposed decision is "adopt framework X" rather than "move toward topology Y"; the Primer names this as the consulting pitch and the cargo-cult source.
Test for single-team scope in an org-change decision

The proposed change touches one team while the surrounding ecosystem is unchanged. "A single high-performing team can't deliver its full potential if surrounding structures and processes remain unchanged."
Test for whole-org-single-design assumption
The proposed design binds the whole company; the Primer names the alternative: different divisions can carry different topologies fitted to different business objectives.
Test for buy-in framing

The word *buy-in* surfaces in the conversation; the LS handbook treats it as a "danger signal telling you that your development and implementation process is missing the essential ingredient of involving all who should be involved."
Classify the situation by complexity (simple/complicated/complex)
Stacey's Agreement/Uncertainty + Zimmerman's recipe/airplane/child distinction; complex decisions are emergent and unpredictable, and recipe/airplane methods fail on them.
Detect "expert-built then bring for buy-in" pattern
"Lack of ownership on the part of the whole community means that the scenarios don't really play a part in decision making." Re-frame as participatory.
Surface the Unusual Suspects upstream
"Who's not here that could contribute to our conversation?" — named as "one of the most important questions you can ask."
Frame the question as wicked when warranted
A paradox or tension between two values the group must hold together; not a problem with a hidden right answer and not a complaint.
Storyboard the engagement-and-decision arc before agenda-fixing
"If your storyboard has a whole lot of podium sessions in a row, it's not going to be very engaging" — diagnose the meeting design before committing to it.
Test for batch-size framing
The question is about *quantity per stage of the value chain* rather than about a single item; per-stage batch size is the decision variable.
Detect a push-based dispatch pattern
Work is being assigned by an upstream dispatcher rather than pulled by team members when they have capacity. Open Kanban's Respect-for-people value asks for pull.
Surface multitasking
Team members are in-flight on multiple items simultaneously; "multitasking does not work" — limit concurrent items per stage.
Detect local optimisation

The proposed improvement is local to one stage of the value chain; the Holistic / Systemic value (Deming + Goldratt) refuses to call stage-local improvements overall improvements without system-throughput evidence.
Test for sustainable pace
The commitment requires *Muri* (overburden) to deliver; "an exhausted developer, manager or team are the perfect recipe for disaster".
Make the work visible before deciding
The work is invisible (no Kanban board, no information radiator); the conversation is on imagined work, not actual work.
Recognise whether the learning conversation is facilitative or transmissive
The first question for any learning engagement: is the learning in the content (transmissive) or in the conversation (facilitative)? Choosing the wrong shape produces wrong artefacts.
Notice the Anxious-Annie pattern in oneself
When the urge to chase inactive participants, over-respond, or intervene early is rising — locate it as one's own pattern, not as participant problem.
Choosing a systems-mapping method based on appropriateness, not familiarity

A project requires a structural map of a complex system and the method choice is open. The decision should triangulate project purpose, system characteristics, and method nature — not default to the familiar method.
Diagnosing a CLD-shaped problem against named archetypes
A system situation has feedback dynamics and resembles a recognisable pattern (vicious cycle, escalation, fix-that-fails, etc.). The archetypes are hypothesis-generators for the core system engine.
Surfacing leverage points in a complex system

A complex system has been mapped (or could be mapped) as a directed cyclic graph; the decision-relevant question is where to intervene. Submaps generated from stakeholder-suggested or system-suggested factors surface system levers and vulnerabilities.
Reasoning about an intervention pathway

An intervention is being designed or evaluated; the decision is about the assumed causal logic from inputs through to long-term impacts. The "fill gaps backward" pattern and "what if no change?" prompt force the assumption surface to the visible.
Testing intervention assumptions probabilistically

The decision involves uncertainty about both the structural pathways and their strengths, and probabilistic reasoning is appropriate. BBNs offer transparent computation without the hidden parameter choices of FCM or System Dynamics.
Simulating intervention dynamics

The decision is about a dynamical sub-problem (not a whole system, not a micro-operational question), and the value lies in playing out the implications of stated assumptions through stocks, flows, and feedback loops.
Reframe the problem statement
The decision feels like it's solving the wrong problem
Identify true root cause
The symptom looks like a resource shortage but may be a process failure
Classify domain complexity
Unsure whether to apply best-practice, expert analysis, experimentation, or stabilising action
Diagnose complex-problem characteristics
Test the situation against the five characteristics (dynamic, multi-stakeholder, time delays, unintended consequences, accumulations) — any one is a signal that linear decision rules will misfire.
Apply the broken-ankle test
If the path forward is obvious, or the decision has to be made immediately, *do not* produce diagrams. Systems-thinking apparatus has overhead; skip it when the situation doesn't warrant it.
Surface the room's mental models before deciding

Each participant holds a partial-but-valid model; partial models in isolation produce ineffective or counterproductive decisions. Ask each participant what they believe is producing the pattern *before* discussing options.
Identify stakeholders affected
Need to consider whose interests are at stake before generating alternatives.
Apply the relevant GAAP principle
The choice involves revenue recognition, expense matching, cost, full disclosure, conservatism, or going-concern application.
Apply the conservatism bias
Estimates involve uncertainty (allowance percentage, contingency probability, useful life, salvage value); the conservative supportable amount is the target.
Account for IFRS divergence
The company reports under both US GAAP and IFRS, or may in future. LIFO, component depreciation, and contingent-liability thresholds materially differ.
Map the budget or production possibilities
Constraints on the decision space (income, capacity, time, regulation) need explicit articulation; corner solutions vs interior tradeoffs.
Recognise increasing opportunity cost
Scaling up an activity will pull resources from less-suitable uses; expect rising marginal cost.
Identify market structure
The applicable profit-maximisation rule (P = MC vs MR = MC) depends on whether competition is perfect, monopolistic, oligopolistic, or monopolistic.
Identify externalities
Third parties bear costs or benefits not internalised in the decision; the private optimum diverges from the social optimum.
Identify information asymmetry
One side knows more than the other; lemons-problem dynamics and signalling devices apply.
Bound the decision with the relevant legal framework
The decision space is being defined without naming the applicable statute, regulation, or common-law standard.
Apply time-value bounds to the financial decision
Multiple alternatives span different time horizons; present-value equivalence is needed before comparison.
Apply the relevant ethical theory constraint
The decision has a hard ethical bound (a Kantian categorical imperative, a rights constraint, a justice-as-fairness floor) that eliminates certain options regardless of expected value.
Bound using the management control cycle
Implementation will require monitoring; the plan-do-check-act cycle sets the bounds of the decision artefact (what will be measured, how often, by whom).
Apply the lean-startup build-measure-learn bound
The decision is resource-limited and reversible; the minimum viable experiment is the correct bound, not the full solution.
Apply the cost-volume-profit constraint
The decision has a volume or pricing dimension; break-even, contribution margin, and operating leverage must be named before alternative evaluation.
Apply the targeting criteria
A segment has been identified but the selection criteria (measurability, substantiality, accessibility, responsiveness) have not been applied.
Apply survival-adjusted ROI for software investment
A software project's payback depends on system lifetime; with annual survival rate 0.79–0.87, software has half-lives in years. Factor in the probability of cancellation in the decision-period ROI.
Apply real-options frame for "we'll invest more later"
The decision is to implement minimum viable functionality with intent to invest later — this is an American call option, not technical debt. What is the cost of the option vs the strike price?
Reject Black-Scholes for software-development valuation
The decision is being framed using financial-options formulas that require liquidity (sale of partial assets) and historical performance — neither holds for software.
Bound by Sprint timebox
An in-Sprint decision needs a timebox — Sprint Planning ≤ 8h, Sprint Review ≤ 4h, Sprint Retrospective ≤ 3h, Daily Scrum = 15 min, Sprint ≤ 1 month.
Anchor on the Sprint Goal
An in-Sprint decision is being made without reference to whether it advances or endangers the Sprint Goal. "No changes are made that would endanger the Sprint Goal" during the Sprint.
Bound by Definition of Done
A "done" judgement is being made case-by-case; the Definition of Done is the pre-committed decision rule that should be applied.
Decompose a multi-action incident into one-action evaluations
Multiple actions or omissions are bound up in a single incident; each must be evaluated through the tree separately, not jointly.
Bound the analysis by the administrative firewall
Without an explicit no-punitive-action commitment in the delegation of authority, honest accounts will not surface. The firewall is non-negotiable; if it's not in writing, the FLA cannot proceed.
Bound the question by separating learning from administrative action
The Delegating Official is asking for findings that will be used administratively; if punishment is the implicit intent, the FLA process should be dropped.
Bound by the reckless-and-willful threshold

A criminal-act question is forming; the threshold (intentional, unjustifiable, foreknowledge of likely serious harm) is high and bright. Below it, FLA continues; at/above, refer to administrative or law enforcement investigation.
Bound the review time-budget honestly
The TC's targets (30–45 min platoon, 1 hr company, 2 hr battalion-and-above) prevent a 30-minute slot pretending to support a 2-hour question. Match duration to depth.
Select the reviewer by experience when in conflict with rank
An evaluator is needed; experience-in-the-role beats rank when the two are in tension.
Apply the two-echelons-above evaluator rule

Independent assessment is needed; the immediate next echelon is too close. The TC's structural rule: two echelons up has enough distance to assess honestly and enough proximity to understand the conditions.
Apply the carry-over rule
If WIP carried over from last Sprint meets or exceeds forecast velocity and is all still valuable, take in zero new work; may even need to remove items. "Honest empirical process control."
Bound by event timebox via sample-agenda segment
The Field Guide breaks each event's timebox into named segments (e.g. 3-hour Sprint Planning into eleven named segments). Use the segment-level allocation to bound the decision moment.
Use a Capacity-adjusted Velocity forecast

A Sprint commitment is being made without quantitative grounding. Apply average-(Velocity/Capacity)-over-last-3-or-4-sprints × forecast Capacity, with the worked example as a sanity check. Fists of Five if Velocity unstable.
Apply 15% Solutions locus-of-control reframe
The decision feels paralysing because the whole context is in play; bound it inward to the 15% of personal/team agency without denying the 85%.
Surface minimum-viable rules via Simple Rules
Five rule categories (How To, Boundary, Priority, Timing, Exit); test by removal — what would happen if you left this rule out?
Bound by the room's physical and aesthetic structure
Space is a structural decision-input, not a logistics detail; no large round tables, natural light, enough elbow room.
Invert with TRIZ before bounding positively
"Design a comprehensive system that makes it absolutely impossible to get that result" — surface self-sabotaging structures before adding positive ones.
Choose the rhythm of rounds to fit the decision
Rhythm is one of the four pattern-language primitives the handbook names; different round-shapes surface different signal.
Bound by stage of the value chain
The same commitment (commit-to-N items) has different costs at different stages; per-stage batch-size discipline applies.
Bound by item complexity (story size)
The proposed item is large (epic-sized); "reduce the number of large stories (epics) you create, and do your best to keep stories simple".
Bound by team-based leadership (not by role restructuring)
"Kanban starts where you are, and does not need to modify any titles or roles in an organization"; the decision space is the existing org, with team leadership activated inside it.
Bound by holistic / systemic improvement target
If the decision optimises one stage at the expense of overall flow, the framework asks for a different decision.
Locate the org-design decision on the OT two axes
The org-change scope is undefined; place it on the Skills mandate (horizontal) / Work mandate (vertical) axes — rightward, upward, or both — before naming the target topology.
Name the systems-thinking dependencies before committing to an org-change
Policies, structures, or rewards are being changed in isolation; the MAP step explicitly warns that optimising elements separately can degrade the system goal ("flow can get worse").
Test single-goal vs multi-goal for the org-change decision
The decision stacks multiple org goals (utilisation, adaptiveness, flow) without naming which is primary. The Primer flags this as a harder problem that must be treated separately, not collapsed.
Set class size and time-budget for the engagement

When sizing a learning engagement: Fundamentals-shape (14–20, 15–25 hrs/wk facilitator), Design-shape, Synchronous-shape (10–20, ~6–8 Practicing Facilitators in a class of 20), MicroCourse-shape (10+ active).
Decide synchronous, asynchronous, or mixed
When choosing the mode for a learning conversation — match mode to learning outcome and participant constraints, not to default.
Vet a tool before adoption
When considering a tool not supported by institution — apply the six-question vetting (purpose, institutional rules, affordances, learner impact, alternatives, content-ownership).
Identify the stock vs the flow

The variable I'm acting on may be a flow rate (signups today, tests written this Sprint) when the relevant decision variable is the stock (waitlist size, technical-debt level, accumulated trust). Bound the decision around the stock, not the flow.
Account for accumulated history
The "blank slate" failure mode — treating a new stakeholder, team, or system state as if it has no history. The state I am bounding is an accumulation of inflows and outflows over time.
Generate enough alternatives
Decision-makers have stopped at two options; richer exploration needed.
Engage diverse perspectives
The team is homogeneous and risks similarity bias; deliberate diversity-seeking needed.
Test for confirmation bias
Decision-makers cannot articulate what evidence would change their view.
Enumerate permissible GAAP treatments
A method-selection decision is in front of you; the alternatives must be explicitly considered, not defaulted.
Test against the matching principle
The choice affects timing of expense or revenue recognition; matching is the GAAP principle that bears most directly.
Surface the auditor's likely view
A judgement call may not survive a fresh audit; engaging the auditor pre-decision is cheaper than post-decision.
Apply comparative advantage at the firm or personal level
The make-or-buy / outsource / partner question turns on opportunity cost, not absolute capability.
Generate at least three alternatives plus a baseline
Comparison space is too narrow; the "do nothing" baseline is missing.
Test for behavioural anomalies
Loss aversion, mental accounting, or fungibility failure may be steering the framing of options.
Surface elasticity
The decision affects price, wage, or fee; the response of the other side determines whether costs can be passed through.
Generate capital-structure alternatives
Financing options are limited to one or two; the full debt/equity/hybrid spectrum plus leverage implications need to be explored.
Enumerate the four IP options
An innovation is ready for protection; all four modes (patent, trademark, copyright, trade secret) and their trade-offs need to be on the table.
Apply the nine business-model-canvas building blocks

A new venture or product is being designed; the full canvas (value proposition, segments, channels, relationships, revenue streams, key resources, activities, partners, cost structure) surfaces hidden constraints.
Run the STP sequence on alternatives
Marketing alternatives are being evaluated without segmenting the market; STP applied to each option clarifies which segment each alternative serves and at what cost-to-reach.
Generate the full competitor-positioning map
The positioning decision is being made without mapping where competitors sit on the key benefit dimensions.
Apply differential analysis to the managerial-accounting decision
A make-or-buy, special-order, or product-mix decision is being evaluated on total cost rather than on incremental (differential) revenues and costs.
Explore ethical lenses on each alternative
Alternatives have been generated; now each needs to be evaluated through at least two ethical lenses (utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, contractarian) to surface hidden moral costs or benefits.
Apply the legal-risk audit to each alternative
Alternatives have been generated; each needs a quick pass through the applicable legal framework (contract, tort, IP, employment, regulatory) to identify dominated options.
Apply the cognitive heuristics and biases checklist
Alternatives have been generated; heuristic-driven biases (availability, representativeness, anchoring, confirmation) may be distorting the comparison.
Generate architecture alternatives using six-comparison frame
Monolith-vs-microservices decision is in front of you; explore each option on communication, deployment, scaling, testing, upgrading, and database.
Choose prototype fidelity for the question
Design decision being explored; low-fidelity paper prototype for concept feedback, medium for accepted-feature feedback, high for detailed-tweak feedback.
Run heuristic evaluation against the eight Inclusivity Heuristics
UI design decision affects diverse users; multiple evaluators independently check the design against the eight Inclusivity Heuristics via Abi/Pat/Tim.
Run Sprint Planning's three Topics to surface options

A Sprint commitment is being formed; Topic One (why valuable / Sprint Goal alternatives), Topic Two (scope alternatives from the Product Backlog), Topic Three (delivery-approach alternatives) is the structured exploration.
Treat forecasting tools as decision inputs, not decision-makers
Burn-downs, burn-ups, and cumulative flows are being used to predict the decision rather than inform it. "What will happen is unknown" in complex environments.
Invite advisors to Sprint Planning when warranted
The Sprint Planning option set is narrow; the team may invite people to provide advice.
Walk the just-culture five-test decision tree in order
An individual action concern is on the table; Q1 (deliberate harm) → Q2 (health) → Q3 (foresight, conjunction) → Q4 (substitution, disjunction) → Q5 (mitigation) applied sequentially.
Apply the foresight test as the system-contract probe
Need to surface whether the system held up its side: is there a protocol; is it workable and in routine use; did the individual knowingly depart? All three Yes to advance.
Apply the substitution test as the peer-and-supervision probe
Need to surface whether peers in the same circumstances would have behaved similarly, whether training was missed, or whether supervision was absent. Any Yes routes to system locus.
Apply the Five Hows to each key decision or action
Need to reach the participants' pre-event situational awareness. "Use those that are helpful and don't waste time trying to cook-book the process."
Decompose beliefs, perceptions, expectations, and paradigms
The team is trying to make sense of decisions that look wrong in hindsight; the four-category decomposition surfaces the pre-event mental models.
Look for the multiple-improbable-events pattern
The analysis is anchoring on a single cause; "most often, the Lessons Learned Analysis will reveal that multiple improbable events were necessary for the accident to occur."
Switch between AAR-style and HRO-style dialogue questions

The AAR-style framing (what was planned / what happened / what worked / what didn't / what to sustain or improve) is missing the high-reliability dimensions; switch to HRO-style questions organised around the five hallmarks (preoccupation with failure, reluctance to oversimplify, sensitivity to operations, commitment to resilience, deference to expertise).
Choose the discussion-organisation technique that fits the next decision

Three options — chronological (narrative recall), BOS-equivalent (systemic patterns across phases), key events (tight focus when time is limited). Pick the organising structure that surfaces the evidence the next decision needs.
Consider the informal-review alternative

The pinecones-and-the-squad-leader 15-minute on-the-spot review with minimal aids often produces more useful retraining decisions than a fully resourced formal review. Is formal scale the right scale?
Use the 5-Whys with "rarely surprised" signal

At a retrospective: ask "why" five times (give or take) until you get to your root cause. "If you're rarely surprised by your root causes, you may not be digging deep enough." Surprise is the signal of progress.
Surface the estimation-vote spread rather than averaging
At Backlog Refinement: a 1-vs-5 spread is information. Explore whether one developer knows a faster technique or another knows a hidden edge case. Don't accept averages or senior-developer opinion.
Generate retrospective candidates with positives/deltas/insights
Process-improvement decision needs candidate alternatives. Each team member writes positives, deltas, and insights individually on sticky notes; group like items; vote on top 3 to deep-dive.
Pre-circulate Backlog Refinement items
Refinement options are narrow because attendees haven't read in. PO sends the list ahead; team familiarises with description, order, value; the event can end early if items reach Ready.
Run 1-2-4-Whole Group on the option space
Individual reflection → pairs → groups of 4-6 → whole group with open question; refuses two-option stopping points.
Use Wise Crowds for option-broadening on a single client's decision

5-6 person consulting team; client describes ~10 min, turns back ≥15 min while team converses; client rejoins with what intrigued/resonated. The team's "job is not to decide 'this is what The Client ought to do.'"
Run Troika when multiple people each have parallel decisions
Three-person, 10-min-per-focal-person rounds; peer honing → between-peer conversation → with-focal-person conversation.
Surface in-system solutions with Discovery & Action Dialogues
Six-question protocol ending with "Who will do what when next?" — surfacing both solutions and volunteers in one structure.
Surface high-value ideas without committing via 25-to-10
Five-round mill-trade-rate-1-to-5; countdown surfaces top-rated. Explicit scope: "not recommended to make a decision."
Surface positional diversity via Mini Constellations
Spatial positioning by degree of agreement; speak from the distance as a "role" — surfaces range without forcing a vote.
Generate the question itself via Q Storming
Post-it brainstorm of provocative and "silly" questions; "Questions are fateful!" — the question generating options matters more than the options.
Probe symptom-vs-cause framing via 5 Whys
Pair-based; ask "WHY?" at least five times; explicitly relaxable beyond five.
Generate batch-reduction alternatives
What's the smallest version of this work that delivers value? Smaller stories, fewer concurrent items per stage.
Generate de-multitasking alternatives
If multiple items are in flight at one stage, which one finishes first if the team focuses on it? "Keeping the team focused helps them finish what they start faster."
Apply the Theory-of-Constraints question
Where is the system constraint, and does this option relax it or shift it? Open Kanban cites Goldratt's ToC explicitly for the holistic frame.
Frame option as value-add or as waste-elimination
Both framings decide the same thing — Open Kanban's Focus-on-Value treats value-creation and *Muda*-elimination as two sides of the same coin; one framing is often easier to commit to than the other.
Consider a two-track design for varied stretch
When the participant population has varied readiness for stretch — designing explicit tracks beats single default with implicit opt-out.
Choose a participatory structure
Weekly studio forums (Design), week-by-week team facilitation (Fundamentals), or 5-step Activity Packet (MicroCourse) — each maps to a different learning-outcome shape.
Design what facilitators will model
Before the engagement begins, pre-decide what practices facilitators will visibly model — lesson plans, interactive sessions, feedback-eliciting activities, reflection.
Generate options widely and equitably
A senior voice will otherwise dominate
Push for creative options
The team is stuck on the obvious answer
Reframe options as experiments
The decision should be tested, not declared
Quick group prioritisation
Time is short and the list is manageable
Relative-value allocation
Need to surface relative weight, not just rank
Scope-cut prioritisation
Some items will explicitly be cut
Effort-aware trade-off
Need to compare options on cost as well as value
Apply the three topologies as starting hypotheses for an org-design decision
For the named business objective, work through the fit logic for each of Resource, Delivery, and Adaptive topology before selecting one. Avoids picking by cultural preference rather than fit.
Explore divergent designs across divisions
The decision is being treated as whole-org; consider whether one division retains Resource Topology (stable legacy work) while another moves to Adaptive (new offerings).
Consider where AI changes the org-design option space
Now that specialisation is "no longer a limited resource," which target archetypes become feasible that previously weren't? Name the AI-enabled options alongside the non-AI options.
Map the feedback loops producing the pattern
Replace the X-causes-Y frame with a structural read: what reinforcing and balancing loops produce the pattern you see? The CLD is a hypothesis to iterate, not an answer to defend.
Test the situation against system archetypes
Five named patterns — Fixes that Fail, Success to the Successful, Shifting the Burden, Drifting Goals, Limits to Growth. Use as starting hypotheses to test, not as labels to apply.
Audit my own and the team's mental-model partiality

None of us has the complete picture; the facilitator's view is partial too. Who is in the room? Whose voice is loudest? Mental models are *especially sticky* among those least exposed to the decision's downside.
Apply critical thinking
Arguments contain logical fallacies (non sequitur, false cause, ad hominem, appeal to tradition) that need to be named.
Use evidence-based methods
A new manager or new domain warrants systematic evidence collection over intuition.
Distinguish process from relationship conflict
Disagreement has descended into personal attack; need to reframe to substantive disagreement.
Document the reasoning for SOX 302/404
Decision will be subject to executive certification or annual control attestation; documentation is the audit trail.
Apply consistency requirement
A method change is being considered; consistency across periods is a GAAP expectation, and changes require disclosure.
Test the front-page (newspaper) test
The choice has an ethical edge; how it would read in *Wall Street Journal* coverage is the appropriate test.
Apply marginal-analysis test
Need to confirm marginal benefit > marginal cost in concrete terms before committing.
Use the right profit-maximisation rule
Production or pricing decision; choose P = MC for competitive context, MR = MC for any pricing power; check break-even and shutdown.
Translate across time
Multi-period decision; convert future costs and benefits to present-discounted value at appropriate rate; check if discount rate reflects rational long-run preferences.
Apply expected value with risk adjustment
Probability-weighted outcome calculation needed; risk preferences applied separately.
Watch for free-rider trap
Collective decision; expectation that others will contribute may be unfounded.
Apply NPV / IRR decision rule
A capital project is being evaluated; NPV > 0 is the correct accept rule; IRR and MIRR are supplementary checks; payback is not sufficient.
Apply the single ethical standard
Multiple ethical frameworks have been applied; now a single standard must be chosen and applied consistently to the final options.
Select the management-strategy tool appropriate to the decision type

A strategic decision is being made; the seven decision-making tools (SWOT, PESTEL, Porter, VRIO, scenario, MBO, balanced scorecard) have different applicability depending on uncertainty and time horizon.
Apply the entrepreneurial pivot criteria

Evidence has accumulated against the current hypothesis; one of the ten pivot strategies (zoom-in, zoom-out, customer segment, customer need, platform, business architecture, value capture, engine of growth, channel, technology) is the structured choice.
Apply contract-formation tests
A contract is being proposed or accepted; offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, and legality all need to be named before committing.
Choose ADR mode
A dispute is being escalated; the choice between mediation, arbitration, and litigation needs an explicit cost-benefit frame (speed, cost, confidentiality, precedent, relationship).
Apply the psychological research quality filter
A claim about human behaviour is being used to justify the decision; the study's design (experimental, correlational, case) determines what causal inference is warranted.
Apply the positioning decision framework
A market position is being selected; the decision requires choosing the value proposition, the points-of-parity and points-of-difference, and the perceptual map location relative to competitors.
Run the keep-or-drop or make-or-buy analysis
A product line, division, or supplier decision is live; relevant-cost analysis (avoidable fixed costs, contribution margin, qualitative factors) determines the financially correct choice.
Run fist of five before commitment
A team-level decision is about to be committed; surface latent dissent (two-or-fewer fingers) before commit.
Estimate via planning poker for sized work
A commitment to a unit of work is needed; private estimate → simultaneous reveal → discuss differences → re-estimate until convergence.
Check user-story quality with INVEST
A user story is being committed; verify Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable.
Finalise the Sprint Goal
The Sprint commitment requires a single, finalised Sprint Goal "communicating why the Sprint is valuable to stakeholders." It "must be finalized prior to the end of Sprint Planning."
Apply the role-specific decision rule

A decision is on the table — who in Scrum has the right? Product Owner (what to build, Sprint cancellation, Product Backlog ordering), Developers (how to build, sizing, Daily Scrum format), team (Sprint Goal definition, team-level Definition of Done).
Test against transparency-inspection-adaptation
After this decision, will the state be visible (transparency)? Will outcomes be inspectable against it? Will the team be empowered to adapt promptly if outcomes diverge?
Select the recommendation end-state from the just-culture tree

The tree has been walked; routing to one of seven recommendations (A deliberate-harm, B substance-abuse, C health-issue, D foresight-branch, E substitution-branch, F mitigation-applies, G residual management action) names the response.
Decide on FLA continuation or termination

The reckless-and-willful disregard threshold has been tested; if met, team leader writes a memo to the Delegating Official terminating the FLA and recommending administrative or law enforcement action — without disclosing details.
Choose a recommendation (Learning Review only)

Recommendations must be vetted through community-of-practice focus groups, evaluated for paradoxical complexity increase, and have a named owner and deadline; the Learning Review Board's approval is the final gate.
Apply train-to-weakness when allocating retraining resources
The default allocation rule for finite development resource is to known weakness, not known strength. Deviation requires a reason.
Stop on critical gate tasks
If a gate task has not been performed to standard, the decision is to retrain, not to progress. Identify gate-task equivalents and honour the stop-rule.
Statistics serve teaching, not grading
If charts are being used to assign verdicts, they have slipped their proper role. Use statistics to identify trends and reinforce teaching points.
Apply the "no firm commitment" decision form

A Sprint commitment is being structured as a firm contract on the Sprint Backlog or Sprint Goal. The decision form is *good-faith forecast and focusing tool*, not contract. Firm commitments produce burnout, poor quality, lost trust, gaming.
Stop adding items when the Development Team — not PO or SM — says so
At Sprint Planning Topic Two: the Development Team decides when there's enough work. PO and SM input is informational, not decisional, on this question.
Split retrospective improvements into decisions vs actions

An improvement is being committed without naming the mechanism. Decisions = changes to Definition of Done, Definition of Ready, or Working Agreement. Actions = new Backlog Items. Different mechanisms register different kinds of commitment.
Stay focused — one or two retrospective improvements per Sprint
The team is proposing many improvements at once. Pick the highest-impact one or two; too much change at once dilutes every experiment.
Pick the confidentiality rule explicitly

A retrospective is about to start without a named confidentiality rule. Vegas Rule (unanimous explicit consent) or Chatham House Rule (information may be shared, identities may not) — choose explicitly.
Recognise LS does not provide a "select the option" structure
The catalogue surfaces and broadens; identify who has legitimate decision authority (Wise Crowds client; Troika focal person; DAD implementer; convening leader).
Use "intrigued/surprised/resonated" for client-side selection
The Wise Crowds re-entry phrase — names what landed without committing to act on everything.
Convert insights into named next steps before adjourning
DAD's "What has to happen next? Who will do what when next?" is the structural commitment that distinguishes a conversation from a decision.
Sequence What/So What/Now What in order
Refuse jumping to Now-What before the What (data) and So-What (pattern) questions have been answered.
Avoid using surfacing tools as committing tools
25-to-10's countdown surfaces; Mini Constellations' clustering surfaces; neither is a decision-rule. Route to a committing tool (DAD's last two questions; Troika's third movement).
Audit the Levels of Accountability of the group
Decisions made from below the line (Wait 'n Hope; Blame self/others; Denial) tend not to be acted on. "What do we need to do to work 'above the line?'"
Apply the pull-based decision rule
The person who pulls is the person who does the work. If someone other than the doer is deciding what to commit, ask why.
Apply the sustainable-pace test
Will the team sustain this across the next several cycles? *Muri* is the named anti-pattern; non-sustainable commitments are decisions the framework refuses.
Apply the batch-size test
Is the chosen scope the smallest viable batch at this stage, or is the team being asked to commit to a larger batch because "we always have"? The default is reduce, not maintain.
Calibrate facilitator presence to phase
High-presence community building early; lighter in participant-led phases; "broad and deep" in mid-course feedback weeks.
Apply the develop-vs-grade default
When designing assessment in a developmental engagement — default to self-assessment via rubric; grading is the explicit opt-in.
Counsel-out a struggling participant
When a participant cannot meet the design's participation requirements — help them decide whether now is the right time, rather than allowing degraded participation.
Apply no-pressuring-tone discipline
When tempted to police deadlines or compliance — adult learners have their own agendas; tone matters; supporting communication produces engagement; policing communication produces disengagement.
Modify approach to cohort capacity
When mid-course pace is creating "do-or-die" atmosphere — the purpose is development, not selection.
Check confidence before commitment
Latent dissent is likely; want to surface it now
Commit without consensus
Forcing agreement would produce compliance, not commitment
Record decision with rationale
The decision needs to be revisitable by future readers
Translate decision to action
Sessions otherwise end without commitment
Review the decision post-execution
Decisions are not closed until reviewed
Design the intervention as loop-structure change
Name what your intervention does to system structure — adds a loop, removes a loop, strengthens or weakens one, decouples a link. If you can't say which, you're operating on the event surface.
Anticipate second-order effects via archetype
What new loops does the chosen intervention create? Will the intervention itself become a *Fixes that Fail* or *Shifting the Burden* in its own right?
Confirm structural-vs-event intervention

Structural interventions are slower, costlier, and produce durable change. Event interventions are faster but require repeated reapplication. Both are legitimate; naming which is which lets the decision-maker choose consciously.
Test strategic alignment of the chosen topology

Does the chosen org goal (flow, adaptiveness, utilisation) actually deliver the capabilities required by the business strategy? The ASSESS step is explicitly about testing the design for strategic alignment.
Name the topology-specific failure mode you are accepting
Every topology has a named failure mode: Resource (narrow-specialist coordination cost), Delivery (feature bloat), Adaptive (high-variance outcomes). Name the one you are accepting before committing.
Test for co-creator vs recipient framing of the org-change
"People, when not owning the change ideas, won't fully accept them." If the impacted population is being told rather than involved, the decision's execution will be degraded.
Manage groupthink risk
A decision is being endorsed unanimously and concerns may be unspoken.
Assign devil's advocate
A high-stakes decision warrants explicit critique before commitment.
Apply internal controls (COSO five components)
Implementation involves transaction recording where opportunity-axis fraud risk applies; segregation of duties, documentation, authorisation, and reconciliation are the controls.
Build the audit trail from source document
A transaction will be subject to external audit; traceability from source through journal, ledger, trial balance, and statement is the structural device.
Communicate marginal logic
Affected parties may resist if the cash framing is presented but the opportunity-cost framing is not; explanation of what is being given up matters.
Pre-commit against future sunk-cost capture
Decision will be defended later regardless of evidence; build sunset clauses, milestones, explicit stop conditions.
Plan for elasticity-driven adjustment
Other parties will substitute or adjust over time as elasticities work through; design for the long-run response, not just the short-run reaction.
Confirm legal compliance before implementation
The decision has been made; a final legal-compliance pass (employment law, contract terms, IP clearance, regulatory filings) is needed before execution.
Communicate to align incentives
The decision is ratified but implementation depends on people who were not in the room; the communication strategy (channel, message, tone-at-the-top) is itself a decision.
Lock in the funding or term-sheet terms
A funding or partnership decision is being committed; the founders' agreement, term-sheet terms, and vesting provisions need to be locked before closing.
Apply the sustainability-reporting framework
The decision will generate externally reported sustainability data; choosing the applicable framework (GRI, SASB, IIRC) is itself a governance decision to make before implementation.
Align the marketing plan for execution
A marketing decision is ratified; the 12-element marketing plan (objectives, STP, 4Ps, budget, controls) needs to be aligned before execution begins.
Lock RACI for the implementation
A decision is ratified; assign Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed roles per task or deliverable before execution.
Set the Definition of Done
A user-story-level decision is ratified; specify the acceptance criteria (often in given-when-then format) that mark the work DONE-done.
Update the relevant Scrum artifact
A Scrum decision is ratified by updating the artifact: Product Backlog (ordering, content), Sprint Backlog (scope, plan, Sprint Goal), or Increment status (Done or not).
Confirm Definition of Done covers the work
Before committing work, does the existing Definition of Done cover it? If multiple teams work on the product, they "must mutually define and comply with the same Definition of Done."
Schedule the next inspection point
A decision without an inspection point has no adaptation loop. When will this decision be inspected — Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective?
Preserve the wider-investigation obligation alongside the individual response
A just-culture decision has reached a recommendation; on every branch, including deliberate-harm, the parallel wider-investigation obligation must be ratified, not displaced.
Couple the just-culture decision to organisational HR and incident reporting policies
The guide is a reference point, not a substitute; senior HR must be engaged in parallel for any individual-management response.
Ratify the learning product with affected participants (readback)

A draft learning product is ready; the readback validates the story with participants in two phases (those directly involved first; supervisors and administrators after). Hard copies are collected afterward.
Lock the Safety Action Plan with owners and deadlines (Learning Review only)
Each approved recommendation needs a named owner and a deadline; the Delegating Official is responsible for follow-up. Without owners and dates, the recommendation does not belong on the plan.
Lock the next-action commitment before closing the review
The TC's principle that benefits live in the follow-up: a review without a next-action commitment and a timeline has not produced its benefit.
Revise SOPs surfaced as broken
If the review surfaced an SOP problem, the SOP must be revised and used in the next iteration; an unrevised SOP after a review that surfaced it is silent regression.
Apply T-P-U as a three-bucket assessment
Trained / Practiced / Untrained is a coarse-grained decision-driving classification, not a five-star rating. Resist the urge to fine-tune the scale.
Capture retrospective improvements in the Sprint Backlog
Improvement experiments are encoded as Backlog Items "to be attempted in the next *Sprint*," not left as informal commitments. The artefact carries the commitment.
Capture Working Agreement / DoD changes in their artefacts
Rule-changes from a retrospective decision register in the Working Agreement (informal) or Definition of Done / Definition of Ready (formal). Without the artefact update, the decision dissolves.
Use the Parking Lot for deferred Daily Scrum decisions
A Daily Scrum decision would exceed the 15-min timebox. Defer to a named Parking Lot with specific participants invited to stay after — not dropped, not held in indefinite limbo.
Anchor on the named next-step language
DAD's "Who will do what when next?"; Wise Crowds' "intrigued/resonated"; World Café's harvest — these are the ratification artefacts.
Plan a sprint of 15% Solutions for distributed ratification
When the decision is too large for any one actor to ratify alone, distribute by 15%-of-control next-step actions per implementer.
Make the Simple Rules explicit and minimal
Five rule categories named; test by removal; strip artefacts.
Widen the implementing population before ratifying
If the implementers are not the same as the decision-makers, the buy-in trap is open.
Make the commitment visible on the board
Has the committed work been placed on the Kanban board or other information radiator? Commitments on invisible work tend not to land.
Honour pull discipline in implementation
Have team members been told to push the new work, or is it in a backlog they can pull from when they have capacity? Push violates pull discipline.
Schedule the learning point
When will the team inspect whether this decision worked — Retrospective, Strategy Meeting, or Kaizen Group? Decisions without an inspection point have no learning loop.
Craft welcome post that sets tone and decision-frame
The first impression — establish presence, clear without being too long or too short, end with invitation.
Lock in pre-course design decisions in the convening agreement
Several-weeks-before / one-to-two-weeks-before / a-few-days-before task lists — pre-course design decisions are made and communicated, not improvised.
Plan for delay-before-signal

Time delays between intervention and result are one of the five characteristics of complex problems. *"Often funding or buy-in is gone long before an initiative can demonstrate impact."* Has the decision-maker secured commitment that exceeds the expected feedback time?
Declare the target topology and archetype set in OT vocabulary
The commitment is being framed in framework language ("we're going SAFe") rather than topology language ("move R&D from CAPS-1 + TASKS-1 to CAPS-3"). Name the target precisely in OT vocabulary.
Frame adopted frameworks as elevation tools, not the decision itself
If the decision involves adopting SAFe, LeSS, or Team Topologies, ratify them as Elevating Kata sources chosen because they help reach the target topology — not as the decision itself.
Commit to incremental elevation and name the first experiments

Avoid big-bang reorganisation framing. "Elevation can be an incremental process." Ratify the first one or two Elevating Katas and the date of the next MAP cycle at the same moment as the topology decision.
Distinguish bad luck from bad decision
Outcome is bad but uncertainty was high; need to evaluate the process separately from the outcome.
Precommit against escalation
A milestone is approaching; decision-maker needs to commit in advance to act on what evidence shows.
Reconcile actual to estimate
Estimate-driven choice (bad debt, useful life, completion percentage) had a quantifiable outcome; significant variance signals the estimate needs revision.
Review for restatement triggers
New facts emerge that suggest a prior choice was incorrect; restatement protects long-term stakeholder trust.
Distinguish bad outcome from bad decision
Outcome is bad; need to identify whether the process was flawed or uncertainty resolved unfavourably.
Check revealed elasticity
Response to the decision did not match assumed elasticity; what does the divergence imply for future modelling?
Reconsider comparative-advantage call
Has another party's opportunity cost shifted, making delegation or trade more attractive than before?
Analyse budget variances
A significant budget variance has surfaced; material price, quantity, efficiency, or spending variance needs root-cause analysis before corrective action.
Apply the balanced scorecard monitoring frame
Financial metrics alone are being used to monitor an implemented decision; the four-perspective balanced scorecard (financial, customer, internal, learning-and-growth) adds leading indicators.
Run the post-implementation review
The plan-do-check-act cycle requires a formal check; compare actual outcomes to the MBO objectives set in Phase 4.
Apply the post-launch metrics
A marketing decision has been implemented; CLV, NPS, brand-equity measures, and customer-acquisition cost need to be tracked against the plan.
Measure against financial projections
A capital project is underway; actual cash flows need to be compared to the NPV model; significant divergence triggers a real-options reassessment.
Monitor lean-startup metrics
A new venture hypothesis is in the field; the build-measure-learn cycle requires actionable metrics that distinguish validated learning from vanity metrics.
Monitor for legal-risk materialisation
A decision with legal exposure has been implemented; periodic review of contract performance, employment compliance, and regulatory status is needed.
Compare actual to estimated effort
Story-points or ideal-days estimates were committed; what did the work actually take? What does the variance imply for velocity adjustment?
Watch code-smell accumulation

Post-decision, are the team's introduced smells (Long Function, Duplicate Code, Inconsistent Conventions) signalling that the original architectural decision was wrong or that refactoring is being deferred?
Detect technical-debt accumulation
"Smelly code leads to smellier code" — is the rate of decay accelerating? Are we deferring refactoring as a normal practice?
Inspect at the Daily Scrum
Daily: are we tracking toward the Sprint Goal? Is the next-day plan actionable? Are impediments being surfaced?
Adapt the Sprint Backlog when conditions change
When work turns out different than expected, are Developers negotiating Sprint Backlog scope with the Product Owner *without affecting the Sprint Goal*?
Inspect outcomes at the Sprint Review
Has the Sprint Review actually been a working session with stakeholders, or degraded to a presentation? Has the Product Backlog been adjusted based on what was learned?
Improve the process at the Sprint Retrospective

What assumptions led the team astray, and what were their origins? What went well, what problems were encountered, and how were they (or weren't they) solved? What's the most impactful change to address next?
Consider Sprint cancellation only if Sprint Goal obsolete
Has the world changed enough that the Sprint Goal is obsolete? Only the Product Owner has authority to cancel.
Watch for partial-implementation drift
Is the team quietly dropping events, watering down accountabilities, or weakening the Definition of Done? "Implementing only parts of Scrum is possible, the result is not Scrum."
Re-enter the just-culture tree when new facts surface
The investigation has surfaced information that would change the answer to one of Q1-Q5; the guide is re-entrant and the routing may now differ.
Run the FLA team's own After-Action Review
The FLA process has completed; the team reconvenes (at least by conference call) to AAR their own experience and forward suggestions to fla.guide.improvements@gmail.com.
Detect drift on a Safety Action Plan

An approved Safety Action Plan is overdue; the Delegating Official is responsible for follow-up; missed deadlines without explanation signal that the recommendation may not have been the right one or that organisational conditions have shifted.
Verify the retraining decision actually happened
If the committed next action has not occurred on the timeline committed, the review has not produced its benefit. Audit the follow-up.
Verify the SOP revision has been implemented
An unrevised SOP after a review that surfaced its problem is silent regression. Has the revised SOP been used in the next iteration?
Re-check T-P-U at the next iteration
A task assessed as Untrained should have moved to Practiced or Trained by the next review. If the assessment has not shifted, the retraining has not produced its effect.
Watch for mature-team delivery range
A mature team delivers 85-115% of planned work or Velocity per Sprint. Consistent under-delivery signals taking on more than historical performance supports.
Don't demonstrate undone work
The Increment isn't Done but the team is about to demonstrate it. The cost is trust erosion plus the illusion that incomplete work is valuable.
Run the meta-retrospective
At the end of the Retrospective: "How did it go for the team? What would they like to try next time?" The retro-on-the-retro is the team's process-of-the-process improvement.
Watch for partial-implementation drift
Events being skipped, accountabilities watered down, DoD weakening — signals of drift away from textbook Scrum. Only deviate as a well-formed experiment.
Run What/So What/Now What as a recurring debrief
At the conclusion of a project phase or sprint; in the named order.
Check the half-life of the decision
The handbook names the half-life problem directly; commitment fades when the structural change has not landed.
Use Appreciative Interviews on the success cases
Pairs interview each other about "a time when…" the decision worked; "what we focus on tends to get bigger."
Watch for the Levels-of-Accountability slide
Blame-direction conversation has dropped below the line; reframe as "What do we need to do to work above the line?"
Test for Q-A-Q-A central-tendency drift in review meetings
One strong voice anchoring the discussion of how the decision is going; batch questions 3-4 at a time; use Take 20 Seconds.
Inspect against system flow, not local efficiency
Has overall flow (lead time, throughput, value delivered) improved, or only stage-local efficiency? The Holistic / Systemic value puts the question at the system level.
Watch for multitasking creep
Has the team's WIP at each stage drifted back up? Batch-size discipline is forward-looking; without monitoring, it lapses.
Watch for partial-implementation drift (Open Kanban)

Has the team quietly dropped one of the four practices — most often the *Learn and improve continuously* learning step? Open Kanban's deliberate incompleteness assumes the four practices are all present.
Adjust the batch-size dial
If overall flow improved with smaller batches, can the team go smaller still? If it worsened, the batch may have been below the work's structural minimum.
Check whether scaffolding has actually faded
By the design's later weeks, is presence actually lighter — use the tracking matrix to check whether facilitator response distribution has shifted toward facilitator-restraint and peer-engagement.
Run post-engagement debrief with co-facilitator
"Don't part company with your co-facilitators without scheduling a debrief" — the debrief is closure, not optional.
Integrate this iteration's lessons into next-iteration design
After each iteration: notes-while-fresh; what to enhance or change; updated convening agreement before next iteration runs.
Watch for archetype repetition across decisions

If decisions keep producing the same surprise pattern, the team may be locked in a recurring archetype (especially Fixes that Fail or Shifting the Burden). Monitor for archetype-shaped repetition, not just individual decision outcomes.
Re-audit the mental models that informed the decision
Mental models shape what gets monitored. If monitoring is producing surprises, the original mental model may have been more partial than recognised; surface and revise.
Re-MAP the organisation against the target archetypes
Has the org actually moved toward the target archetypes, or did the change stall at announcement? "Periodic re-mapping with Org Topologies is an easy and fast way to complete a learning loop."
Test for drift back toward Resource Topology under predictability pressure
Resource is the cultural default; utilisation-as-default pressure is constant. Drift back shows in re-MAP as movement toward CAPS-1 / TASKS-level archetypes after an elevation commitment.
Surface new bottlenecks after elevation
AI-driven elevation dissolves expertise constraints and surfaces coordination ones. After a successful elevation cycle, re-apply the three AI-investment questions to the *next* binding bottleneck.
